Interface Redesign · 2026

From scattered
to intentional

A homepage redesign for Kullvi Whims — a Himalayan craft brand whose soul was always present. It just needed room to show itself.

BrandKullvi Whims
TypeE-commerce Redesign
ScopeHomepage
MethodHeuristic Evaluation + Figma
Scroll
The Brand

A mountain village.
A living craft.

Kullvi Whims is a social enterprise based in Naggar, Himachal Pradesh. They work with women artisans in the Kullu Valley — sourcing indigenous Himalayan wool from nomadic shepherds, dyeing it with local flora, then spinning, knitting, and weaving. Every piece is the result of hands that have carried this craft for generations.

This is not a brand that needs to manufacture meaning. The meaning is already there — in the material, the mountains, the people. The website's job is simply to not get in the way of it.

"The wool already carries the story. The design just needs to stay honest to it."

The Problem

The brand had soul.
The homepage had too much to say.

The existing site wasn't broken. The photography was rich, the copy was heartfelt, the products were beautiful. But the homepage tried to hold everything at once — and left first-time visitors with no single clear impression to leave with.

01
No centre of gravity
Every section carried equal visual weight. Nothing anchored the eye or directed the visitor forward.
02
Commerce buried mid-scroll
A first-time visitor encountered the full brand narrative before a single product category.
03
Trust signals scattered
Shipping and craft credentials were distributed across the footer — not placed near where a visitor decides whether to stay.
04
Navigation through scrolling
No visual category entry points on the homepage. Ready-to-buy visitors had to work before discovering the range.
The Approach

I audited the site.
Then sent it to them.

Before designing anything, I ran the existing homepage through a structured review using Nielsen's heuristic principles — not as a checklist, but as a lens. I was looking for the gap between what the brand is and what the interface was communicating to a first-time visitor.

Once the audit was complete, I packaged it as a formal proposal and reached out to Kullvi Whims directly. The redesign grew from that audit — structured around four concrete refinement pillars.

Four Pillars of Refinement
01
Establish product intent within 5 seconds. First-time visitors should understand what is being sold before any story is told.
02
Introduce commerce earlier, without disrupting tone. Seasonal products moved ahead of the craft narrative.
03
Consolidate trust before action. Craft credentials clustered near the primary decision point — not spread across the footer.
04
Clarify navigation for ready buyers. A visual category grid surfaces the full range at a glance.
Key Decisions

Five choices that
changed the feeling.

01 —
The hero kept its video — but the overlay changed
The live site uses a Kullu Valley workshop video as its hero background — one of its strongest assets. The redesign keeps this. What changes is the text treatment around it: a category label above the headline, a subheading that names the product explicitly, and a trust line below the CTA. The video does the emotional work; the type does the structural work.
Visual Hierarchy
Before Live Site
  • Emotion-first headline — product category implied, not stated
  • Subheading is atmospheric, not descriptive
  • Single CTA with no surrounding trust signals
After Kullvi Whims Interface Redesign
  • "Himalayan Knitwear" label anchors the category above the headline
  • Subheading names the product and makers explicitly
  • Trust line below CTA: Small-batch · Ships Worldwide · Secure Checkout
02 —
Products moved before the story
In the original, the brand narrative appeared between the hero and the product carousel — the first scroll brought a wall of brand text. In the redesign, a "Crafted for the Season" product row comes immediately after the hero, catching high-intent visitors before they've decided whether to stay. The brand story follows, given more visual weight through a dedicated dark section. Neither section was removed; only the order changed.
Page Architecture
Before Live Site
  • Brand narrative block appears immediately after hero
  • Products only visible after scrolling past the full story text
  • High-intent visitors must scroll before reaching anything to click
After Kullvi Whims Interface Redesign
  • "Crafted for the Season" product row sits directly below the hero
  • High-intent visitors reach browsable products on the first scroll
  • Brand narrative follows, given a dedicated dark section with more visual weight
03 —
"From Fleece to Fabric" became a section, not a paragraph
The original compressed the entire supply chain story into a single centred text block on a light background. In the redesign it becomes three image pillars on a dark ground: Women Artisans of Kullu, Indigenous Himalayan Wool, Natural Dyeing & Regional Flora. The same information, now shown rather than just told. Each pillar builds trust in a different dimension of the brand.
Information Architecture
Before Live Site
  • Full supply chain story compressed into one centred paragraph
  • Visually flat — no images, no hierarchy within the section
  • The depth of the narrative is hidden inside the text
After Kullvi Whims Interface Redesign
  • Three image pillars: artisans, wool, dyeing — each given visual space
  • Dark background creates strong contrast with the product sections around it
  • The story is shown, not just described — each pillar builds a different dimension of trust
04 —
Category grid replaced the scrolling carousel
The original used a horizontal product carousel — a format that hides most of what's available and requires effort to discover. The redesign introduces a visual category grid: Jackets, Knitwear, Shawls & Stoles, Poncho, Accessories, Home Collection, Tara Collection. A visitor can see the full world of the brand in a single glance. Show the map, don't make users wander through it.
Navigation Design
Before Live Site
  • Horizontal carousel shows 3–4 categories at a time
  • The rest of the range is hidden — requires clicking to discover
  • No category labels or hierarchy to help orient a new visitor
After Kullvi Whims Interface Redesign
  • 7-category visual grid: all categories visible at once
  • Category labels and short descriptions sit within each image
  • High-intent visitors can orient themselves and navigate directly
05 —
A closing line instead of a closing section
The original homepage ended with a generic footer — customer service hours, newsletter signup, social links. Functional, but tonally flat after everything that preceded it. The redesign closes with a single typographic statement: "Every thread carries a story of landscape, labor, and lineage." In a scroll-heavy interface, the last thing a visitor reads is what they carry with them.
Copywriting & Pacing
Before Live Site
  • Page ends with customer service info and newsletter form
  • Functional, but tonally disconnected from the brand voice above
  • Nothing to carry away — the experience ends on a utility note
After Kullvi Whims Interface Redesign
  • "Every thread carries a story of landscape, labor, and lineage."
  • Single typographic statement on a dark ground — the brand's voice, not a form
  • The experience closes on the same emotional register it opened on

Interface Refinements

The smaller things
that add up.

Beyond the structural changes, a set of consistent smaller decisions brought the interface closer to the quality of the product itself.

—
Consistent spacing rhythm
Irregular vertical gaps between sections made the original feel assembled rather than designed. A more consistent system creates visual flow as you scroll.
—
Stronger typographic hierarchy
Heading sizes and weights adjusted to create clear distinction between section titles, product names, and supporting text.
—
Product card consistency
Cards follow a consistent grid structure. Titles, prices, and image crops are aligned — making the catalogue easier to scan and compare.
—
Clearer price visibility
At ₹10,000–₹22,000 price points, pricing needs to feel intentional. Improved contrast and positioning make it easier to find.
—
Narrative and commerce separated
Story-driven sections are visually distinct from product browsing areas. When separated, each can do its specific job without diluting the other.
—
Reduced visual clutter
Competing promotional banners were removed. The breathing room lets the photography — which is already excellent — do more of the selling.
"

The job of a brand interface is not to impress. It's to get out of the way of the thing that already is impressive.

Design conviction · Kullvi Whims Redesign · 2026
Reflection

What I learned
doing this.

What worked

Starting with the brand rather than the interface. The first question wasn't "what does this homepage need?" — it was "what kind of company is this, and what should a first-time visitor feel when they leave?" That question made every subsequent decision simpler. When you know what you're protecting, you know what to remove.

Using heuristics as a diagnostic language rather than a checklist. Nielsen's principles didn't tell me what to design — they gave me words for what I'd already sensed intuitively. The mismatch between the site's warmth and its structural clarity was always visible. The framework helped me articulate it clearly enough to share with someone else.

Sending the audit directly to the brand. That step turned this from a personal exercise into something I could stand behind. It also forced me to be specific and honest — you can't be vague when you're addressing a real founder about their real business.

What I'd do differently

This was a visual redesign based entirely on what I could observe. I made assumptions about where visitors lost interest and how they moved through the page — but I had no way to verify them. In a real project, I'd want to watch a few recorded user sessions and talk to at least two or three existing customers before deciding what to change.

I'd also extend the scope to the individual product page — where purchase decisions actually happen. The homepage sets the tone, but the product page closes the sale. Getting that full journey right is the work this redesign points toward, not the work it completes.

And I'd iterate more explicitly — documenting what I tried, what I rejected, and why. The five decisions here look clean because I've described them as decisions. In practice there were more drafts, more dead ends, and more uncertainty than the final version suggests.

A note on context

This is a self-initiated concept project. I don't have a live implementation to point to or conversion data to cite. What I have is an honest account of how I thought through a real problem for a real brand — the frameworks I applied, the instincts I followed, and the limits I'm clear about. I think that's a more useful signal than a case study that makes the process look cleaner than it was.

Neelabh Srivastava
Kullvi Whims Redesign · 2026
Self-initiated concept project.
Not affiliated with or commissioned by Kullvi Whims.
